Shutters.name: Shutters
Shutters.description: Shutters are physical objects removed and secured by Fezziwig and his apprentices to prepare the warehouse for celebration.

Lamps.name: Lamps
Lamps.description: Lamps in the warehouse are trimmed to prepare the space for the festive gathering.

Fire.name: Fire
Fire.description: The fire in the warehouse is heaped with fuel to ensure the room remains warm and inviting during the celebration. | The Fire is a source of warmth and light in the miners' hut, around which the family gathers, symbolizing comfort and celebration amid hardship.

Music Book.name: Music Book
Music Book.description: A book carried by the fiddler to organize and guide the musical performance at the celebration.

Sir Roger de Coverley.name: Sir Roger de Coverley
Sir Roger de Coverley.description: Sir Roger de Coverley is a traditional English country dance tune played by the fiddler to conclude the festive evening.

Porter.name: Porter
Porter.description: Porter is a type of dark beer served in a pot, specifically provided for the fiddler to cool his face during the celebration.

Cold Roast.name: Cold Roast
Cold Roast.description: Cold Roast is a large dish of roasted meat served at the festive gathering.

Cold Boiled.name: Cold Boiled
Cold Boiled.description: Cold Boiled is a large dish of boiled meat served alongside the Cold Roast at the celebration.

Mince-Pies.name: Mince-Pies
Mince-Pies.description: Mince-pies are small sweet pastries served as part of the festive food offerings. | Mince-pies are festive food items associated with Christmas celebrations, mentioned as part of the lavish feast surrounding the Ghost of Christmas Present.

Negus.name: Negus
Negus.description: Negus is a warm spiced drink made with wine, water, sugar, and spices, served during the celebration.

Dance.name: Dance
Dance.description: The dance is a lively social event featuring multiple couples, forfeits, and structured movements, hosted by Fezziwig.

Forfeits.name: Forfeits
Forfeits.description: Forfeits are playful penalties or games played during the dance, adding amusement to the festivities.

Beer.name: Beer
Beer.description: Beer is a beverage served in abundance during the celebration, accompanying the food and dance.

The Two Apprentices.name: The Two Apprentices
The Two Apprentices.description: The two apprentices are young workers under Fezziwig who remain after the ball and express heartfelt admiration for their former employer.

The Ghost of Christmas Past.name: The Ghost of Christmas Past
The Ghost of Christmas Past.description: The Ghost of Christmas Past is a supernatural entity that guides Scrooge through memories of his past, prompting reflection on his life and choices.

Christmas Ball.name: Christmas Ball
Christmas Ball.description: The Christmas ball is a festive gathering hosted by Fezziwig, featuring traditional dances and warm social interactions among employees and their families.

Advance and Retire.name: Advance and Retire
Advance and Retire.description: Advance and retire is a dance step performed during the Christmas ball, part of the traditional social dancing of the time.

Cork-Screw.name: Cork-Screw
Cork-Screw.description: Cork-Screw is a dance move performed during the Christmas ball, known for its spinning motion and skillful execution by Fezziwig.

Thread-the-Needle.name: Thread-the-Needle
Thread-the-Needle.description: Thread-the-Needle is a dance maneuver performed during the Christmas ball, involving partners weaving between each other in a specific pattern.

Happiness Through Small Gestures.name: Happiness Through Small Gestures
Happiness Through Small Gestures.description: Happiness through small gestures is the idea that joy and fulfillment can be created through seemingly insignificant acts of kindness and personal presence, as demonstrated by Fezziwig.

The Fair Young Girl.name: The Fair Young Girl
The Fair Young Girl.description: Scrooge's former fiancée, who ends their engagement because he has replaced her with the pursuit of wealth.

Gain.name: Gain
Gain.description: The dominant passion that has taken root in Scrooge, replacing his earlier aspirations and personal relationships with an obsession for wealth.

Poverty.name: Poverty
Poverty.description: A state of economic hardship that Scrooge once accepted but now fears, and which he believes the world wrongly condemns when contrasted with the pursuit of wealth. | Poverty is a condition observed by the Spirit, portrayed not as destitution but as a state rich in hope and resilience.

The Pursuit of Wealth.name: The Pursuit of Wealth
The Pursuit of Wealth.description: The driving force in Scrooge's life that has led to the erosion of his personal relationships and moral values.

The Contract.name: The Contract
The Contract.description: The informal agreement between Scrooge and his former fiancée to marry after improving their financial situation through patient industry.

The Girl.name: The Girl
The Girl.description: The girl is a former love of Scrooge who releases him from their bond, citing his transformation and materialistic values. | The Girl is one of two children emerging from the Ghost's robe, depicted as wretched, ragged, and deformed, symbolizing Want and the consequences of societal neglect. | The girl is Fred's servant who answers the door for Scrooge and assists him in locating Fred in the dining room.

The Beautiful Young Girl.name: The Beautiful Young Girl
The Beautiful Young Girl.description: A young girl who grows into a comely matron, mother to several children, and central to the joyful family scene.

The Comely Matron.name: The Comely Matron
The Comely Matron.description: The former young girl, now a mother, who laughs heartily and enjoys the chaotic play of her children.

The Daughter.name: The Daughter
The Daughter.description: The daughter of the comely matron, who participates in the children's playful chaos and is the object of Scrooge's nostalgic longing.

The Father.name: The Father
The Father.description: The father who returns home, greeted enthusiastically by his family, and arrives with Christmas toys and presents.

The Porters.name: The Porters
The Porters.description: A man who accompanies the father and is overwhelmed by the children's exuberant reception upon arrival.

The Children.name: The Children
The Children.description: A group of children whose boisterous, individualistic behavior creates a tumultuous yet joyful atmosphere.

Christmas Toys and Presents.name: Christmas Toys and Presents
Christmas Toys and Presents.description: Items brought home by the father, symbolizing holiday generosity and the center of the children's excited rush.

Braided Hair.name: Braided Hair
Braided Hair.description: A personal feature of the daughter, held in deep sentimental value by Scrooge as a symbol of innocence and beauty.

The Precious Little Shoe.name: The Precious Little Shoe
The Precious Little Shoe.description: A small item belonging to the daughter, representing childhood purity and the object of Scrooge's protective longing.

The Flushed and Boisterous Group.name: The Flushed and Boisterous Group
The Flushed and Boisterous Group.description: The collective moment of chaotic joy when the children rush to greet the father, embodying the emotional climax of the scene.

Playful Chaos.name: Playful Chaos
Playful Chaos.description: The unrestrained, joyful, and disorderly behavior of the children, which is celebrated rather than reprimanded by the adults.

Nostalgia.name: Nostalgia
Nostalgia.description: Scrooge's deep emotional longing for the innocence, touch, and freedom of childhood, as observed in the family scene.

The Husband.name: The Husband
The Husband.description: The husband is Belle's spouse, who shares with her a memory of encountering Scrooge alone in his office.

The Partner.name: The Partner
The Partner.description: The Partner is Scrooge's deceased or dying business associate, mentioned as lying on the point of death.

The Baby.name: The Baby
The Baby.description: The Baby is a child in the family scene who is playfully mischievous, having put a doll's frying pan in his mouth and been suspected of swallowing a toy turkey.

The Doll's Frying Pan.name: The Doll's Frying Pan
The Doll's Frying Pan.description: A small toy object used by the baby in a playful but alarming incident during the family gathering.

The Fictitious Turkey.name: The Fictitious Turkey
The Fictitious Turkey.description: A toy turkey, glued to a wooden platter, that the baby is suspected of swallowing during the festive scene.

The Wooden Platter.name: The Wooden Platter
The Wooden Platter.description: A wooden base to which the fictitious turkey is glued, part of the baby's mischievous act.

The Parlour.name: The Parlour
The Parlour.description: The parlour is the room where the family's festive gathering occurs before the children move upstairs.

The Top of the House.name: The Top of the House
The Top of the House.description: The uppermost level of the house where the children go to bed after the celebration.

Scrooge's Office.name: Scrooge's Office
Scrooge's Office.description: The location where Scrooge is seen alone, illuminated by a candle, reflecting his isolation and emotional desolation. | Scrooge's office is a workplace that he once occupied but later finds occupied by another, signifying his loss of identity and legacy.

The Fireside.name: The Fireside
The Fireside.description: The warm, central hearth where the husband, Belle, and her mother sit together in a moment of familial affection.

Irrepressible Affection.name: Irrepressible Affection
Irrepressible Affection.description: The overwhelming, uncontainable display of love and playfulness shown by the children toward their father, contrasting with Scrooge's solitude.

Joy, Gratitude, and Ecstasy.name: Joy, Gratitude, and Ecstasy
Joy, Gratitude, and Ecstasy.description: Collective emotional states experienced by the family during the gift-opening scene, described as indescribable yet profound.

Shadows of the Things That Have Been.name: Shadows of the Things That Have Been
Shadows of the Things That Have Been.description: The spectral visions shown by the Ghost, representing past events from Scrooge's life that he is forced to confront.

Extinguisher-Cap.name: Extinguisher-Cap
Extinguisher-Cap.description: The Extinguisher-Cap is a physical object used by Scrooge in an attempt to suppress the light of the Ghost, but which fails to fully conceal its glow.

The Second Spirit.name: The Second Spirit
The Second Spirit.description: The Second Spirit is a supernatural messenger sent to Scrooge following Jacob Marley's intervention, anticipated with alertness and readiness by Scrooge.

The Bell Striking One.name: The Bell Striking One
The Bell Striking One.description: The event when the clock strikes one, triggering Scrooge's fear and the appearance of a mysterious light in his room.

The Ruddy Light.name: The Ruddy Light
The Ruddy Light.description: A mysterious, glowing light that fills Scrooge's room, causing him anxiety and uncertainty about its origin and meaning.

The Adjoining Room.name: The Adjoining Room
The Adjoining Room.description: The room adjacent to Scrooge's, suspected by him to be the source of the mysterious light.

The Transformed Room.name: The Transformed Room
The Transformed Room.description: Scrooge's room after a supernatural transformation, adorned with living greenery, gleaming berries, and festive foods.

Mistletoe.name: Mistletoe
Mistletoe.description: A plant with glossy leaves and berries, used decoratively in the transformed room. | Mistletoe is a parasitic plant associated with Christmas traditions, mentioned among the festive decorations. | Mistletoe is a decorative plant associated with Christmas, which vanishes during the magical transformation. | Mistletoe is hung up in the shop, serving as a decorative element associated with the Christmas season.

Ivy.name: Ivy
Ivy.description: A climbing plant whose leaves contribute to the lush, green decoration of the transformed room. | Ivy is a climbing plant used in Christmas decorations, mentioned alongside holly and mistletoe. | Ivy is a decorative plant associated with Christmas, which vanishes during the magical transformation.

Turkeys.name: Turkeys
Turkeys.description: Birds piled on the floor as part of a lavish feast in the transformed room. | Turkeys are large birds traditionally eaten during Christmas feasts, listed among the festive meats. | Turkeys are festive food items associated with Christmas, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Geese.name: Geese
Geese.description: Birds included among the festive foods arranged on the floor in the transformed room. | Geese are birds commonly served at Christmas dinners, included in the array of holiday foods. | Geese are festive food items associated with Christmas, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Game.name: Game
Game.description: Wild animals hunted for food, displayed as part of the feast in the transformed room. | Game refers to wild animals hunted for food, associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Poultry.name: Poultry
Poultry.description: Domesticated birds, such as chickens or ducks, part of the abundance of food in the transformed room. | Poultry refers to domesticated birds raised for food, associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Brawn.name: Brawn
Brawn.description: A type of cooked meat, likely from pork, displayed as part of the feast in the transformed room. | Brawn is a type of meat preparation, associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Great Joints of Meat.name: Great Joints of Meat
Great Joints of Meat.description: Large cuts of meat arranged on the floor as part of the festive spread in the transformed room.

Sucking-Pigs.name: Sucking-Pigs
Sucking-Pigs.description: Young pigs roasted whole, presented as part of the feast in the transformed room.

Long Wreaths of Sausages.name: Long Wreaths of Sausages
Long Wreaths of Sausages.description: Decorative arrangements of sausages forming wreaths, part of the food display in the transformed room.

Minced Pies.name: Minced Pies
Minced Pies.description: Small baked pastries filled with minced meat or fruit, included in the feast in the transformed room.

Plum Puddings.name: Plum Puddings
Plum Puddings.description: Traditional dense desserts, part of the festive food arrangement in the transformed room.

Barrels of Oysters.name: Barrels of Oysters
Barrels of Oysters.description: Containers holding oysters, displayed as part of the abundance of food in the transformed room. | Barrels of oysters are festive food items, part of the rich array of holiday delicacies surrounding the Ghost of Christmas Present.

Red-Hot Chestnuts.name: Red-Hot Chestnuts
Red-Hot Chestnuts.description: Chestnuts roasted and steaming, contributing to the warmth and aroma of the transformed room. | Red-hot chestnuts are warm, seasonal treats, mentioned as part of the aromatic and steam-filled Christmas feast.

Cherry-Cheeked Apples.name: Cherry-Cheeked Apples
Cherry-Cheeked Apples.description: Apples with a reddish hue, displayed as part of the festive fruit selection in the transformed room. | Cherry-cheeked apples are vividly described fruits, symbolizing abundance and festivity in the Christmas scene.

Juicy Oranges.name: Juicy Oranges
Juicy Oranges.description: Oranges, noted for their freshness and color, included in the feast displayed in the transformed room. | Juicy oranges are luxurious seasonal fruits, part of the bountiful Christmas spread.

Luscious Pears.name: Luscious Pears
Luscious Pears.description: Pears described as rich and ripe, part of the fruit display in the transformed room. | Luscious pears are rich, ripe fruits included in the array of festive foods surrounding the Ghost of Christmas Present.

Immens Twelfth-Cakes.name: Immens Twelfth-Cakes
Immens Twelfth-Cakes.description: Large celebratory cakes, traditionally associated with Twelfth Night, placed on the floor as part of the feast.

Seething Bowls of Punch.name: Seething Bowls of Punch
Seething Bowls of Punch.description: Large bowls of hot alcoholic beverage, bubbling and filling the room with steam and aroma. | Seething bowls of punch are steaming, alcoholic holiday beverages that contribute to the warm, aromatic atmosphere of the scene.

The Chimney.name: The Chimney
The Chimney.description: The fireplace in Scrooge's room, from which a mighty blaze roars, contrasting with its previously dull state.

Plum-Puddings.name: Plum-Puddings
Plum-Puddings.description: Plum-puddings are traditional Christmas desserts, described as part of the abundant holiday feast in the scene.

Twelfth-Cakes.name: Twelfth-Cakes
Twelfth-Cakes.description: Immense twelfth-cakes are large, celebratory cakes associated with the Twelfth Night of Christmas, featured in the feast.

Glowing Torch.name: Glowing Torch
Glowing Torch.description: A glowing torch held by the Ghost of Christmas Present, shaped like Plenty's horn, used to illuminate Scrooge and symbolize abundance.

Holly Wreath.name: Holly Wreath
Holly Wreath.description: A holly wreath worn on the head of the Ghost of Christmas Present, adorned with shining icicles, symbolizing the Christmas season.

Antique Scabbard.name: Antique Scabbard
Antique Scabbard.description: An ancient scabbard girded around the Ghost of Christmas Present's middle, empty and rusted, suggesting the absence of violence.

Red Berries.name: Red Berries
Red Berries.description: Red berries are decorative elements found on holly and mistletoe, contributing to the visual richness of the Christmas scene. | Red berries are decorative elements of Christmas, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Meat.name: Meat
Meat.description: Meat is a food item associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Pigs.name: Pigs
Pigs.description: Pigs are animals raised for meat, associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Sausages.name: Sausages
Sausages.description: Sausages are prepared meat products associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Oysters.name: Oysters
Oysters.description: Oysters are seafood delicacies associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Pies.name: Pies
Pies.description: Pies are baked food items associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Puddings.name: Puddings
Puddings.description: Puddings are dessert items associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Fruit.name: Fruit
Fruit.description: Fruit is a food item associated with Christmas feasts, which vanish during the magical transformation.

Punch.name: Punch
Punch.description: Punch is a festive drink associated with Christmas, which vanishes during the magical transformation.

Christmas Morning.name: Christmas Morning
Christmas Morning.description: Christmas morning is the time setting where Scrooge finds himself after the magical transformation, marked by snowy streets and communal activity.

Great Britain.name: Great Britain
Great Britain.description: Great Britain is the geographic region referenced in the description of chimneys emitting sooty smoke.

Spanish Onions.name: Spanish Onions
Spanish Onions.description: Spanish onions are described as ruddy, brown-faced, and broad-girthed, shining with fatness and appearing to wink slyly at passersby.

Pears.name: Pears
Pears.description: Pears are clustered in blooming pyramids, displayed prominently among other fruits in the grocer's shop.

Apples.name: Apples
Apples.description: Apples are clustered in blooming pyramids, displayed prominently among other fruits in the grocer's shop.

Grapes.name: Grapes
Grapes.description: Grapes are arranged in bunches and hung from conspicuous hooks to entice customers with their visual appeal.

Filberts.name: Filberts
Filberts.description: Filberts are mossy and brown, emitting a fragrance that evokes memories of autumn walks through wooded areas.

Norfolk Biffins.name: Norfolk Biffins
Norfolk Biffins.description: Norfolk Biffins are squab and swarthy fruits that contrast with oranges and lemons and are described as urgently inviting to be purchased.

Oranges.name: Oranges
Oranges.description: Oranges are displayed alongside Norfolk Biffins, their yellow color enhanced by the contrast with darker fruits.

Lemons.name: Lemons
Lemons.description: Lemons are displayed alongside Norfolk Biffins, their yellow color enhanced by the contrast with darker fruits.

Gold Fish.name: Gold Fish
Gold Fish.description: Gold fish are kept in a bowl among the fruits, appearing to show passive excitement despite their sluggish nature.

Grocers.name: Grocers
Grocers.description: The Grocers refer to the shopkeepers and their establishment, where an abundance of seasonal goods are displayed and sold.

Tea.name: Tea
Tea.description: Tea is one of the blended scents in the grocer's shop, contributing to the aromatic atmosphere.

Coffee.name: Coffee
Coffee.description: Coffee is one of the blended scents in the grocer's shop, contributing to the aromatic atmosphere.

Raisins.name: Raisins
Raisins.description: Raisins are described as plentiful and rare, contributing to the visual and sensory appeal of the shop.

Almonds.name: Almonds
Almonds.description: Almonds are extremely white, highlighting their purity and quality among the displayed goods.

Cinnamon Sticks.name: Cinnamon Sticks
Cinnamon Sticks.description: Cinnamon sticks are long and straight, contributing to the visual and aromatic richness of the shop.

Other Spices.name: Other Spices
Other Spices.description: Other spices are described as delicious, enhancing the sensory allure of the grocer's display.

Candied Fruits.name: Candied Fruits
Candied Fruits.description: Candied fruits are caked and spotted with molten sugar, creating an enticing visual and tactile appeal.

Figs.name: Figs
Figs.description: Figs are moist and pulpy, adding to the rich variety of fruits on display.

French Plums.name: French Plums
French Plums.description: French plums are depicted as blushing with modest tartness, displayed in highly-decorated boxes.

Bakers' Shops.name: Bakers' Shops
Bakers' Shops.description: Bakers' shops are locations where people bring their dinners on Christmas Day, and where the Spirit interacts with dinner-carriers.

Christmas Day.name: Christmas Day
Christmas Day.description: Christmas Day is a festive occasion marked by communal dining, church attendance, and the expression of goodwill among people. | Christmas Day is the festive occasion on which the Cratchit family gathers for a modest but joyful celebration centered around the goose and family reunion. | Christmas Day is the festive occasion on which the Cratchit family gathers for a meal and reflects on themes of compassion and gratitude. | Christmas Day is the occasion around which the Cratchit family gathers, marked by a strained toast to Scrooge and a subsequent return to merriment. | Christmas Day is the day on which Scrooge awakens transformed, realizing he has not missed the holiday and deciding to act with kindness.

Incense.name: Incense
Incense.description: Incense is a substance sprinkled by the Spirit from his torch to restore good humor and promote kindness among people.

Torch.name: Torch
Torch.description: The torch is a magical device wielded by the Spirit that emits incense and water to influence human emotions and behavior on Christmas Day.

Dinner-Carriers.name: Dinner-Carriers
Dinner-Carriers.description: Dinner-carriers are individuals who transport meals to bakers' shops on Christmas Day and are recipients of the Spirit's benevolent intervention.

Good Humour.name: Good Humour
Good Humour.description: Good humour is a state of kindness and cheerfulness that the Spirit cultivates among people, especially during conflicts on Christmas Day.

Pavement Smoke.name: Pavement Smoke
Pavement Smoke.description: Pavement smoke is a visual phenomenon above baker's ovens, indicating the warmth and progress of cooking dinners on Christmas Day.

Spirit.name: Spirit
Spirit.description: The Spirit is a supernatural being associated with Christmas, capable of invisibility, size adaptation, and blessing homes; it confronts Scrooge about social injustice. | The Spirit is a supernatural entity that accompanies Scrooge on a journey, revealing scenes of human life and emotion, and is associated with compassion and presence in places of suffering. | The Spirit is a supernatural entity guiding Scrooge through visions of how others perceive his death, serving as a moral guide.

Master Peter Cratchit.name: Master Peter Cratchit
Master Peter Cratchit.description: Master Peter Cratchit is Bob Cratchit's son, who wears his father's old shirt collar on the holiday and takes pride in his appearance. | Master Peter Cratchit is the eldest son of the Cratchit family, who is exalted by his younger siblings and is responsible for tending the fire during the Christmas preparations.

Christmas Present.name: Christmas Present
Christmas Present.description: Christmas Present represents the spirit of generosity, compassion, and celebration during the Christmas season, embodied by the Spirit.

Seventh Day.name: Seventh Day
Seventh Day.description: The Seventh Day refers to Sunday, the traditional day of rest and worship, which Scrooge is accused of wanting to deprive the poor of.

Fifteen Shillings.name: Fifteen Shillings
Fifteen Shillings.description: Fifteen shillings is the weekly wage earned by Bob Cratchit, symbolizing his impoverished condition despite his family's dignity.

Twice-Turned Gown.name: Twice-Turned Gown
Twice-Turned Gown.description: A twice-turned gown is a worn-out dress altered to extend its use, worn by Mrs. Cratchit to maintain appearances on a limited budget.

Ribbons.name: Ribbons
Ribbons.description: Ribbons are inexpensive decorative items used by Mrs. and Belinda Cratchit to create a festive, respectable appearance despite poverty.

Monstrous Shirt-Collar.name: Monstrous Shirt-Collar
Monstrous Shirt-Collar.description: A large, outdated shirt collar belonging to Bob Cratchit, given to his son Peter as a symbolic gift for the holiday.

The Cratchit Children.name: The Cratchit Children
The Cratchit Children.description: The two younger Cratchit children, a boy and a girl, are energetic and excited, eagerly announcing the smell of the goose and participating in the festive preparations. | The Cratchit children include Master Peter, Miss Belinda, and two unnamed young Cratchits who assist in preparing and serving the Christmas meal.

Bob's Monstrous Shirt-Collar.name: Bob's Monstrous Shirt-Collar
Bob's Monstrous Shirt-Collar.description: Bob's monstrous shirt-collar is a piece of clothing passed down to him as a family heirloom, worn with pride on Christmas Day despite its size and discomfort.

The Goose.name: The Goose
The Goose.description: The goose is the central Christmas meal prepared by the Cratchit family, whose aroma is detected by the children outside the baker's shop. | The goose is the central dish of the Cratchit family's Christmas meal, prepared with great care and excitement. | The goose is the central dish of the Christmas meal, admired for its tenderness, flavor, size, and cheapness.

Sage and Onion.name: Sage and Onion
Sage and Onion.description: Sage and onion are aromatic ingredients used in the preparation of the Christmas goose, evoking anticipation and delight among the Cratchit children. | Sage and onion are used as stuffing for the goose, so abundant that the youngest Cratchits are described as being 'steeped' in them.

The Iron Frame.name: The Iron Frame
The Iron Frame.description: The iron frame is a supportive device used to assist Tiny Tim in standing and walking, indicating his physical disability.

The Comforter.name: The Comforter
The Comforter.description: The comforter worn by Bob Cratchit is a large, thick scarf, described as having at least three feet of fringe, symbolizing his effort to appear seasonably dressed.

Martha.name: Martha
Martha.description: Martha is a member of the Cratchit family who emerges from behind a closet door to comfort someone on Christmas Day.

The Pudding.name: The Pudding
The Pudding.description: The pudding is cooked in a copper pot and is described as 'singing,' indicating it is a cherished part of the Christmas tradition. | The pudding is a Christmas dessert prepared by Mrs. Cratchit, described as hard, firm, and blazing with ignited brandy, decorated with holly. | The pudding is a central dish of the Cratchit Christmas dinner, symbolizing family tradition and the social expectation of abundance despite limited resources.

The Hot Mixture.name: The Hot Mixture
The Hot Mixture.description: The hot mixture is a drink made by Bob Cratchit from gin and lemons, simmered on the hob as part of the Christmas celebration.

The Carving-Knife.name: The Carving-Knife
The Carving-Knife.description: The carving-knife is used by Mrs. Cratchit to serve the goose, and its use is preceded by a moment of suspense. | The carving-knife is used by Mrs. Cratchit to cut into the goose, marking the moment of anticipation before serving.

The Crutch.name: The Crutch
The Crutch.description: Tiny Tim's crutch is a physical aid that he uses, and its sound on the floor signals his return to the family gathering.

The Church.name: The Church
The Church.description: The church is referenced by Tiny Tim as a place where he hopes people will see him on Christmas Day to remember divine miracles.

The Wash-House.name: The Wash-House
The Wash-House.description: The wash-house is where Tiny Tim is taken so he cannot hear the pudding cooking until it is time to return.

The Table.name: The Table
The Table.description: The table is where the Cratchit family gathers for their Christmas meal, with Tiny Tim seated beside Bob.

The Fire.name: The Fire
The Fire.description: The fire is the heating source beside which Tiny Tim is seated after returning from the wash-house. | The fire is the central point around which the family gathers, symbolizing warmth, togetherness, and comfort during their evening conversation.

The Copper.name: The Copper
The Copper.description: The copper is the large pot in which the pudding is boiled, producing a singing sound as it cooks. | The copper is the cooking vessel from which the pudding is removed, emitting steam and aromatic smells.

The Saucepan.name: The Saucepan
The Saucepan.description: The saucepan is used by Mrs. Cratchit to prepare the gravy beforehand.

The Apple Sauce.name: The Apple Sauce
The Apple Sauce.description: The apple sauce is sweetened by Miss Belinda as part of the Christmas meal preparation.

The Gravy.name: The Gravy
The Gravy.description: The gravy is made by Mrs. Cratchit and served hot with the goose.

The Pots and Pans.name: The Pots and Pans
The Pots and Pans.description: Collective kitchen utensils used in preparing the Christmas meal, including the jug, saucepan, and copper.

Apple Sauce.name: Apple Sauce
Apple Sauce.description: Apple sauce is served alongside the goose to supplement the meal.

Mashed Potatoes.name: Mashed Potatoes
Mashed Potatoes.description: Mashed potatoes are served as a side dish to extend the goose meal for the family.

Christmas Holly.name: Christmas Holly
Christmas Holly.description: Christmas holly is used decoratively, stuck into the top of the pudding to enhance its festive appearance.

Brandy.name: Brandy
Brandy.description: Brandy is ignited and used to flame the pudding, creating a dramatic visual and aromatic effect during serving.

The Cloth.name: The Cloth
The Cloth.description: The cloth is the wrapping used to steam the pudding, contributing to its distinctive smell upon removal.

The Cratchit Family.name: The Cratchit Family
The Cratchit Family.description: The Cratchit family is a poor but loving household that gathers around the hearth to celebrate Christmas with simple food and drink, embodying warmth and resilience.

Christmas Dinner.name: Christmas Dinner
Christmas Dinner.description: The Christmas dinner is a modest family meal prepared by the Cratchits, featuring a pudding, apples, oranges, and chestnuts, and followed by a heartfelt toast.

Christmas Toast.name: Christmas Toast
Christmas Toast.description: The Christmas toast is a ritual in which Bob Cratchit and his family express goodwill and blessings, culminating in Tiny Tim's words, 'God bless us every one!'

The Jug.name: The Jug
The Jug.description: The jug contains a hot beverage served to the Cratchit family, tasted and deemed perfect, and poured into makeshift glassware during the Christmas celebration.

The Glassware.name: The Glassware
The Glassware.description: The glassware consists of two tumblers and a custard cup without a handle, used by the Cratchit family to serve drinks, symbolizing their humble means.

Chestnuts.name: Chestnuts
Chestnuts.description: Chestnuts are roasted on the fire during the Cratchit Christmas celebration, adding auditory and aromatic warmth to the gathering. | Roasted nuts served during the family's Christmas evening, symbolizing simple domestic comfort.

Penitence and Grief.name: Penitence and Grief
Penitence and Grief.description: Penitence and grief are the emotional responses Scrooge experiences upon hearing his own harsh words echoed by the Ghost, marking a turning point in his character.

Five-and-sixpence.name: Five-and-sixpence
Five-and-sixpence.description: Five-and-sixpence is the weekly income projected for Peter Cratchit's potential new job, representing a significant financial improvement for the family.

Milliner's.name: Milliner's
Milliner's.description: The milliner's is the workplace where Martha Cratchit serves as an apprentice, subject to long working hours.

Countess.name: Countess
Countess.description: A noblewoman observed by Martha Cratchit, mentioned briefly as part of her experience outside the home. | A noblewoman seen by the family some days before, mentioned in conversation as a point of interest.

Lord.name: Lord
Lord.description: A nobleman observed by Martha Cratchit, mentioned alongside the countess in her account of social encounters. | A nobleman seen by the family, noted for being approximately the same height as Peter.

Peter.name: Peter
Peter.description: Peter is a member of a humble family who reacts to a comment about a lord's height by pulling up his collar, and may be familiar with pawnbrokers. | Peter is one of the Cratchit children who reads aloud from a book, likely the Bible, to the quiet family. | Peter is one of the Cratchit children who observes his father's changed walking pace and recalls memories of Tiny Tim. | Peter is one of Bob and Mrs. Cratchit's children, who is teased about possibly finding a romantic partner and securing a better job.

A Lost Child Travelling in the Snow.name: A Lost Child Travelling in the Snow
A Lost Child Travelling in the Snow.description: A song performed by Tiny Tim, depicting a child lost in snowy conditions, sung with a plaintive voice.

Christmas Holiday.name: Christmas Holiday
Christmas Holiday.description: A day off from work observed by the family at home, marked by togetherness, song, and simple pleasures.

Pawnbroker's.name: Pawnbroker's
Pawnbroker's.description: A place associated with financial hardship, possibly familiar to Peter, indicating the family's economic struggles.

Jug.name: Jug
Jug.description: A vessel used to serve drinks, passed around during the family's gathering along with chestnuts.

Roaring Fires.name: Roaring Fires
Roaring Fires.description: Fires in kitchens and parlours that provide warmth and light, emblematic of domestic celebration and hospitality.

Deep Red Curtains.name: Deep Red Curtains
Deep Red Curtains.description: Curtains ready to be drawn to exclude cold and darkness, indicating preparation for a cozy evening.

Hot Plates.name: Hot Plates
Hot Plates.description: Plates heated before the fire in preparation for a dinner, signifying domestic care and anticipation.

Hooded and Fur-Booted Girls.name: Hooded and Fur-Booted Girls
Hooded and Fur-Booted Girls.description: A group of elegantly dressed young women who walk together to a neighbor's house, described playfully as 'artful witches.'

Ghost.name: Ghost
Ghost.description: A benevolent supernatural being that exults in the joy of human connection and generosity during Christmas.

The Lamplighter.name: The Lamplighter
The Lamplighter.description: The Lamplighter is a minor character who runs ahead, lighting street lamps and laughing as the Ghost passes, unaware of the Ghost's presence.

The Miners.name: The Miners
The Miners.description: The Miners are a group of people living and working in the bowels of the earth, who celebrate Christmas with song and family despite their harsh environment.

The Old Man.name: The Old Man
The Old Man.description: The Old Man is a miner who sings a traditional Christmas song to his family, gaining energy from their participation in the chorus. | The old man is a deceased individual whose corpse is plundered by others after death; he was disliked in life and is remembered with contempt.

The Moors.name: The Moors
The Moors.description: The Moors are a bleak, desolate landscape with rudimentary stone formations, frozen water, and sparse vegetation, serving as a setting for the miners' dwelling.

The Sea.name: The Sea
The Sea.description: The Sea is a vast, violent body of water that Scrooge witnesses after leaving the moors, characterized by thundering waves and destructive power.

Christmas Song.name: Christmas Song
Christmas Song.description: A traditional Christmas song sung by the old miner and his family, passed down through generations and central to their festive gathering.

Moss.name: Moss
Moss.description: Moss is a plant that grows on the barren moors, one of the few forms of vegetation surviving in the harsh environment.

Furze.name: Furze
Furze.description: Furze is a coarse, spiny shrub that grows on the moors, alongside rank grass, as one of the few forms of plant life in the desolate region.

Coarse Rank Grass.name: Coarse Rank Grass
Coarse Rank Grass.description: Coarse rank grass is a type of vegetation found on the moors, growing in the harsh, frozen environment alongside moss and furze.

Hut.name: Hut
Hut.description: The Hut is a simple dwelling made of mud and stone, where the miners and their families gather to celebrate Christmas by the fire.

Lamplight.name: Lamplight
Lamplight.description: Lamplight is the artificial light produced by the lamplighter, dotting the dusky street with small points of illumination as he performs his evening duties.

The Lighthouse.name: The Lighthouse
The Lighthouse.description: A solitary lighthouse built on a reef of sunken rocks, where two men keep a fire and celebrate Christmas with grog and song.

The Ship.name: The Ship
The Ship.description: A ship at sea where all crew members, despite their circumstances, share Christmas thoughts, tunes, and kindness toward one another.

Grog.name: Grog
Grog.description: Grog is a drink consumed by the two men at the lighthouse during their Christmas celebration.

Seaweed.name: Seaweed
Seaweed.description: Seaweed clings to the base of the lighthouse and is described as being born of the water, contrasting with storm-birds born of the wind.

Storm-Birds.name: Storm-Birds
Storm-Birds.description: Storm-birds are avian creatures that rise and fall around the lighthouse, likened to the waves they skim.

The Wind.name: The Wind
The Wind.description: The wind is a natural force that creates storm-birds and moans over the sea, contributing to the atmosphere of the scenes observed by Scrooge.

Scrooge's Niece.name: Scrooge's Niece
Scrooge's Niece.description: Scrooge's niece is a pretty, earnest, and expressive woman who strongly disapproves of Scrooge's attitude toward Christmas and shares her husband's sentiments. | Scrooge's niece is an enthusiastic participant in the gathering, encouraging her brother Fred to continue speaking and expressing agreement with his views on Scrooge. | Scrooge's niece plays the harp and performs a simple tune that evokes memories of Scrooge's childhood, prompting emotional reflection. | Scrooge's niece is seated comfortably during the party, participates in forfeits, and excels at the game 'How, When, and Where.'

Laughter.name: Laughter
Laughter.description: Laughter is portrayed as an irresistible and contagious force of good humor that unites Scrooge's nephew, niece, and their friends in celebration.

Wealth.name: Wealth
Wealth.description: Wealth refers to Scrooge's financial resources, which are criticized for being unused and unshared, depriving others of potential benefit.

Topper.name: Topper
Topper.description: Topper is a bachelor who is visibly attracted to one of Scrooge's niece's sisters and responds dismissively when asked for his opinion on bachelors. | Topper is a member of the musical family who sings bass well and participates in blind man's buff, showing deliberate and humorous behavior toward the plump sister. | Topper is a guest at the party who knows that Scrooge's sisters are sharp players.

Scrooge's Niece's Sister (Plump, with La.name: Scrooge's Niece's Sister (Plump, with Lace Tucker)
Scrooge's Niece's Sister (Plump, with La.description: One of Scrooge's niece's sisters, described as plump with a lace tucker, who blushes when Topper expresses his views on bachelors.

Scrooge's Niece's Sister (With Roses).name: Scrooge's Niece's Sister (With Roses)
Scrooge's Niece's Sister (With Roses).description: One of Scrooge's niece's sisters, distinguished from the plump sister by having roses, and not the focus of Topper's attention.

Dinner.name: Dinner
Dinner.description: Dinner is a social gathering at which Scrooge's family is assembled, serving as a context for expressing opinions about Scrooge's isolation.

Mouldy Old Office.name: Mouldy Old Office
Mouldy Old Office.description: The mouldy old office is Scrooge's workplace, described as a lonely and uninviting place where he spends his time.

Dusty Chambers.name: Dusty Chambers
Dusty Chambers.description: The dusty chambers are Scrooge's private living quarters, portrayed as isolated and cheerless, contrasting with the warmth of the family gathering.

The Plump Sister.name: The Plump Sister
The Plump Sister.description: The plump sister is a participant in the blind man's buff game, targeted specifically by Topper, and protests that the game is unfair.

The Simple Little Air.name: The Simple Little Air
The Simple Little Air.description: A simple musical tune played on the harp by Scrooge's niece, which triggers memories of Scrooge's childhood and his time at the boarding school.

Glee.name: Glee
Glee.description: A type of musical piece performed by the family, showcasing their musical talent and festive spirit.

Catch.name: Catch
Catch.description: A type of round or canon song performed by the musical family during their evening entertainment.

Harp.name: Harp
Harp.description: A musical instrument played by Scrooge's niece, used to perform the tune that evokes Scrooge's memories.

Boarding-School.name: Boarding-School
Boarding-School.description: The place where Scrooge was sent as a child, referenced by the Ghost of Christmas Past and recalled when the simple tune is played.

Blind Man.name: Blind Man
Blind Man.description: The Blind Man participates in a game of blind man's buff and has a confidential interaction with a woman behind the curtains.

Plump Sister.name: Plump Sister
Plump Sister.description: The Plump Sister is pursued by the Blind Man during the game of blind man's buff and protests that the game is unfair. | The plump sister is a participant in the guessing game who correctly identifies Scrooge as the answer and joins in the celebration.

Yes and No.name: Yes and No
Yes and No.description: Yes and No is a party game where one person thinks of an object and others ask yes-or-no questions to guess it.

How, When, and Where.name: How, When, and Where
How, When, and Where.description: How, When, and Where is a party game in which Scrooge's niece excels, outperforming her sisters.

Live Animal.name: Live Animal
Live Animal.description: The live animal is the subject of the 'Yes and No' game, described as disagreeable, savage, and one that growls and grunts.

London.name: London
London.description: London is the city where Scrooge lives and moves about, not confined to public displays or institutions.

Happy New Year.name: Happy New Year
Happy New Year.description: Happy New Year is a celebratory occasion mentioned alongside Christmas, invoked in the toast to Scrooge.

Mulled Wine.name: Mulled Wine
Mulled Wine.description: Mulled wine is a warm, spiced drink served during the celebration, used in the toast to Scrooge's health.

Almshouse.name: Almshouse
Almshouse.description: An almshouse is a charitable residence for the poor, visited by the Spirit, where human suffering is met with compassion.

Hospital.name: Hospital
Hospital.description: A hospital is a place of healing visited by the Spirit, where the sick are depicted as cheerful despite their condition.

Gaol.name: Gaol
Gaol.description: A gaol (jail) is a place of confinement visited by the Spirit, symbolizing areas where society's marginalized are found.

Vain Man's Little Brief Authority.name: Vain Man's Little Brief Authority
Vain Man's Little Brief Authority.description: Vain man's little brief authority refers to human governance or control that fails to exclude the Spirit from places of suffering.

Guessing Game.name: Guessing Game
Guessing Game.description: The guessing game is a social activity in which participants deduce Scrooge’s identity through elimination, culminating in a joyful revelation.

Toasting.name: Toasting
Toasting.description: Toasting is a ritual act of raising a drink to honor someone, performed by Fred and his guests in honor of Scrooge.

The Boy.name: The Boy
The Boy.description: The Boy is one of two children emerging from the Ghost's robe, depicted as wretched, ragged, and deformed, symbolizing Ignorance and the consequences of societal neglect.

Twelfth-Night Party.name: Twelfth-Night Party
Twelfth-Night Party.description: A Christmas-related celebration that Scrooge and the Ghost attend, marking a moment when Scrooge notices the Ghost's physical aging.

Ignorance.name: Ignorance
Ignorance.description: Ignorance is symbolized by the boy emerging from the Ghost's robe, representing societal neglect and the lack of education or awareness among the poor. | Ignorance is personified as a boy, representing societal neglect and lack of education, and is identified by the Spirit as the greater threat among the two children.

Want.name: Want
Want.description: Want is symbolized by the girl emerging from the Ghost's robe, representing poverty, deprivation, and the material suffering of the underprivileged. | Want is personified as a girl, representing poverty and deprivation, and is presented alongside Ignorance as a consequence of societal failure.

The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come.name: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come
The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come.description: The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come is a spectral entity that appears as a shrouded, silent phantom, showing Scrooge visions of future events. | The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come is a silent, ominous specter that shows Scrooge visions of the future, particularly his own death and isolation.

Spirit of the Future.name: Spirit of the Future
Spirit of the Future.description: The Spirit of the Future is a spectral entity that appears as a silent, shrouded figure with a pointing hand, intended to show Scrooge visions of his future.

Great Fat Man.name: Great Fat Man
Great Fat Man.description: A merchant at Change who is indifferent to Scrooge's death and only knows that he died last night.

Red-Faced Gentleman.name: Red-Faced Gentleman
Red-Faced Gentleman.description: A merchant at Change with a distinctive nose, who inquires about what Scrooge did with his money after his death.

Black Gloves.name: Black Gloves
Black Gloves.description: Black gloves are symbolic attire associated with mourning, mentioned by a speaker who claims disinterest due to never wearing them.

Lunch.name: Lunch
Lunch.description: Lunch is referenced as a condition for attending Scrooge's funeral, revealing the transactional and indifferent attitude toward his death.

Business Point of View.name: Business Point of View
Business Point of View.description: Business point of view refers to the narrow, professional perspective by which Scrooge valued his relationships, devoid of personal warmth.

Jacob.name: Jacob
Jacob.description: Jacob was Scrooge's old partner, whose death is referenced as a past event unrelated to the Spirit's current focus on the future.

The Phantom.name: The Phantom
The Phantom.description: The Phantom is a silent, ominous presence beside Scrooge, characterized by its outstretched hand and unseen eyes that observe him intently. | A spectral figure guiding Scrooge through visions of the future, pointing silently to the dead man's body as a warning.

The Porch.name: The Porch
The Porch.description: The Porch is an entrance point to a bustling area where Scrooge expects to see his own image but finds none, symbolizing his anticipated transformation.

The Obscure Part of Town.name: The Obscure Part of Town
The Obscure Part of Town.description: An impoverished, crime-ridden district with foul alleys, wretched buildings, and desperate inhabitants, where Scrooge has never been before but recognizes its reputation.

The Low-Browed Shop.name: The Low-Browed Shop
The Low-Browed Shop.description: A dilapidated shop in the impoverished district where discarded iron, rags, bones, and other refuse are bought and stored.

Rusty Keys.name: Rusty Keys
Rusty Keys.description: Rusty keys are among the discarded metal objects collected in the low-browed shop, symbolizing forgotten or abandoned access and ownership. | Discarded metal keys found in piles within Old Joe's shop, part of the assortment of refuse he deals in.

Nails.name: Nails
Nails.description: Nails are discarded metal fasteners found among refuse in the low-browed shop, indicative of decay and discarded utility. | Discarded nails present among the refuse items in Old Joe's shop.

Hinges.name: Hinges
Hinges.description: Hinges are broken mechanical components found among refuse, symbolizing decayed connections or structures. | Broken or discarded hinges, part of the refuse iron collection in Old Joe's shop.

Files.name: Files
Files.description: Files are worn metal tools found among refuse, indicating discarded utility and labor. | Metal files, worn and rusty, among the items sold by Old Joe.

Scales.name: Scales
Scales.description: Scales are weighing instruments found among refuse, possibly symbolizing lost fairness or measurement. | Old weighing scales, likely used for measuring scrap metal, present in Old Joe's shop.

Weights.name: Weights
Weights.description: Weights are metal objects used for measurement, found among refuse, suggesting abandoned balance or order. | Metal weights, part of the refuse iron and discarded tools in Old Joe's shop.

Refuse Iron.name: Refuse Iron
Refuse Iron.description: Refuse iron is scrap metal collected in the shop, representing discarded value and economic decay. | General term for discarded iron objects of various kinds collected and sold by Old Joe.

Future.name: Future
Future.description: The Future is the domain of the Spirit, contrasted with the past death of Jacob, and is the focus of Scrooge's introspection and anticipated moral lessons.

Moral Improvement.name: Moral Improvement
Moral Improvement.description: Moral improvement is the intended outcome Scrooge believes will arise from observing the Spirit's signs and his future self's conduct.

Crime.name: Crime
Crime.description: Crime is a pervasive condition in the obscure part of town, associated with filth, misery, and degradation.

Filth.name: Filth
Filth.description: Filth describes the physical and moral decay evident in the impoverished district, manifesting in dirt, smell, and squalor.

Misery.name: Misery
Misery.description: Misery characterizes the suffering of the people in the impoverished district, reflected in their appearance and living conditions.

Old Joe.name: Old Joe
Old Joe.description: An elderly man, nearly seventy years old, who deals in discarded items and operates a shop filled with refuse. He is described as a grey-haired rascal who smokes a pipe and has a casual, familiar relationship with his customers. | Old Joe is a receiver of stolen goods who appraises and pays for items taken from the deceased man's residence. | Old Joe is a character who buys stolen items from women after a man's death, and is described as being overly generous yet pragmatic about his dealings.

The Charwoman.name: The Charwoman
The Charwoman.description: A woman who entered Old Joe's shop first with a heavy bundle, identified by Mrs. Dilber as the charwoman. She is the first to arrive and initiates the group's recognition.

The Undertaker's Man.name: The Undertaker's Man
The Undertaker's Man.description: A man in faded black who arrives at Old Joe's shop shortly after the two women. He is startled by the others' presence and is identified by Mrs. Dilber as the undertaker's man.

Old Joe's Shop.name: Old Joe's Shop
Old Joe's Shop.description: A cluttered, dimly lit space filled with heaps of rusty keys, nails, chains, refuse iron, rags, corrupted fat, and bones. It includes a parlour area screened off by tattered curtains and a charcoal stove made of old bricks.

Unseemly Rags.name: Unseemly Rags
Unseemly Rags.description: Tattered, dirty fabrics piled in Old Joe's shop, used to screen off the parlour area and mixed with other refuse.

Corrupted Fat.name: Corrupted Fat
Corrupted Fat.description: Decomposed or spoiled organic fat matter found among the refuse in Old Joe's shop, suggesting long-term accumulation of waste.

Bones.name: Bones
Bones.description: Human or animal bones, described as sepulchres, found among the refuse in Old Joe's shop.

Charcoal Stove.name: Charcoal Stove
Charcoal Stove.description: A stove made of old bricks, used by Old Joe for warmth in his shop.

Frouzy Curtaining.name: Frouzy Curtaining
Frouzy Curtaining.description: A makeshift curtain made of miscellaneous tatters hung on a line to screen off the parlour area from the shop's cold air.

Smoky Lamp.name: Smoky Lamp
Smoky Lamp.description: An oil lamp in Old Joe's shop that is dim and smoky, requiring trimming with the stem of his pipe.

Stair-Rod.name: Stair-Rod
Stair-Rod.description: An old stair-rod repurposed by Old Joe to rake together the fire in his stove.

The Deceased Man.name: The Deceased Man
The Deceased Man.description: A deceased man, described as a 'wicked old screw,' whose possessions are being divided and sold by others after his death. | The deceased man is the owner of the stolen items, including bed-curtains and a shirt, whose body was left unattended and robbed after death. | The deceased man is the husband of Caroline and the creditor whose death brings relief to his debtors, though his identity is not explicitly named.

The Bundle.name: The Bundle
The Bundle.description: A bundle containing items taken from the deceased man's residence, including a seal, pencil-case, sleeve-buttons, and a brooch.

Sheets and Towels.name: Sheets and Towels
Sheets and Towels.description: Household textiles taken from the deceased man's residence and later appraised by Old Joe.

Silver Teaspoons.name: Silver Teaspoons
Silver Teaspoons.description: Two old-fashioned silver teaspoons taken from the deceased man's belongings and appraised by Old Joe.

Sugar-Tongs.name: Sugar-Tongs
Sugar-Tongs.description: A household utensil taken from the deceased man's residence and appraised by Old Joe. | Sugar-tongs are small household utensils among the stolen items sold to Old Joe.

Boots.name: Boots
Boots.description: Footwear taken from the deceased man's possessions and appraised by Old Joe. | Boots are personal footwear items taken from the deceased man and sold to Old Joe.

Moral Justification.name: Moral Justification
Moral Justification.description: The belief held by the characters that taking the deceased man's belongings is not sinful because he neglected others in life.

Appraisal.name: Appraisal
Appraisal.description: The process by which Old Joe evaluates the value of stolen items and records prices on the wall.

Chalked Prices.name: Chalked Prices
Chalked Prices.description: The recorded monetary values written on the wall by Old Joe for each item taken from the deceased man's residence.

The First Woman.name: The First Woman
The First Woman.description: The first woman is one of the individuals who stole belongings from a deceased man, including bed-curtains and a shirt, and justifies her actions with cold pragmatism.

The Shirt.name: The Shirt
The Shirt.description: The shirt is the best clothing belonging to the deceased man, which was stolen by the woman to prevent it from being buried with him.

Oil.name: Oil
Oil.description: Oil is a substance that the woman warns Old Joe not to spill on the blankets, indicating its potential to damage the stolen items.

Blankets.name: Blankets
Blankets.description: Blankets are items stolen from the deceased man's bed, which the woman is concerned about being soiled by oil.

Calico.name: Calico
Calico.description: Calico is a type of fabric mentioned by the woman as sufficient for burying the deceased man, implying it is a low-cost, common material.

The Bed.name: The Bed
The Bed.description: A bare, uncurtained bed on which the deceased man's body lies, uncovered and unattended, symbolizing neglect and isolation.

The Flannel Bag.name: The Flannel Bag
The Flannel Bag.description: A flannel bag containing money taken from the deceased man's possessions, used by others to distribute their gains.

Death.name: Death
Death.description: Death is personified as a force of dominion and dread, representing the finality and moral weight of the old man's lonely end.

Avarice.name: Avarice
Avarice.description: Avarice is the dominant trait associated with the old man and potentially Scrooge, representing greed and the corrosive effect of selfishness. | Avarice is the dominant trait associated with the dead man, representing greed and selfishness that led to his lonely end.

The Ragged Sheet.name: The Ragged Sheet
The Ragged Sheet.description: A ragged sheet covering the body of the deceased man, symbolizing neglect and the absence of care or dignity in death.

The Dead Man.name: The Dead Man
The Dead Man.description: The dead man is a figure who lived a life of avarice and isolation, with no one to mourn him or recall his kindness.

Caroline's Husband.name: Caroline's Husband
Caroline's Husband.description: Caroline's husband is a young, careworn man who delivers the news that the dead man has died and will not relent on his debts.

The Room of Death.name: The Room of Death
The Room of Death.description: The Room of Death is an empty, dark house where the dead man lies unattended, surrounded by restless animals.

The Kitchen.name: The Kitchen
The Kitchen.description: The kitchen is the room where Caroline and her children wait, and where her husband returns home with news of the dead man's death.

Relenting.name: Relenting
Relenting.description: Relenting refers to the possibility of the dead man changing his harsh stance on debts, which is now impossible due to his death.

Rats.name: Rats
Rats.description: Rats are animals gnawing beneath the hearthstone, symbolizing decay and neglect in the room of the dead man.

Cat.name: Cat
Cat.description: A cat is tearing at the door of the room where the dead man lies, adding to the sense of disturbance and desolation.

Cratchit's Wife.name: Cratchit's Wife
Cratchit's Wife.description: Cratchit's wife is the mother of the Cratchit children and is emotionally affected by the loss of Tiny Tim, as indicated by her reaction to candlelight. | Cratchit's wife is a supportive and resilient mother who manages the household and comforts her family, especially after Bob's emotional outburst.

The Half-Drunk Woman.name: The Half-Drunk Woman
The Half-Drunk Woman.description: The half-drunk woman is a minor character who informed Caroline about the dying state of the deceased man, which was initially dismissed as an excuse.

And He Took a Child, and Set Him in the.name: And He Took a Child, and Set Him in the Midst of Them
And He Took a Child, and Set Him in the.description: A biblical passage read aloud by Peter, symbolizing themes of innocence, care, and loss in the context of Tiny Tim's death.

The Two Young Cratchits.name: The Two Young Cratchits
The Two Young Cratchits.description: The two young Cratchits are children who comfort their father by placing their cheeks against his face, showing affection and empathy. | The two young Cratchits are children in the Cratchit family who express affection for their father and share in the family's emotional moments.

The Room Above.name: The Room Above
The Room Above.description: The room above is a cheerfully lit, Christmas-decorated space where Bob Cratchit goes to grieve and reconcile with the loss of Tiny Tim.

Sunday Walk.name: Sunday Walk
Sunday Walk.description: The Sunday walk is a promised ritual Bob Cratchit made to visit a green place in memory of Tiny Tim, which he fulfilled on the day described.

Mr. Scrooge's Nephew.name: Mr. Scrooge's Nephew
Mr. Scrooge's Nephew.description: Mr. Scrooge's nephew is a kind and compassionate gentleman who showed concern for Bob Cratchit and offered assistance, leaving a strong impression on him.

The Girls.name: The Girls
The Girls.description: The girls are Bob and Mrs. Cratchit's daughters, who participate in the family conversation and express emotional solidarity with their father.

The Card.name: The Card
The Card.description: The card is given by Mr. Scrooge's nephew to Bob Cratchit, serving as a tangible symbol of his offer of support and kindness.

The Grave.name: The Grave
The Grave.description: The grave is a physical site in a churchyard where Scrooge is shown the final resting place of a man he ultimately recognizes as himself. | The grave is a neglected burial site marked with Ebenezer Scrooge's name, serving as the pivotal vision that triggers his emotional transformation.

Churchyard.name: Churchyard
Churchyard.description: The churchyard is a burial ground surrounded by houses and overgrown with vegetation, symbolizing neglect and the inevitability of death.

The Bedpost.name: The Bedpost
The Bedpost.description: The bedpost is the physical object into which the Ghost collapses, symbolizing the end of the supernatural vision and Scrooge's return to reality.

Christmas Time.name: Christmas Time
Christmas Time.description: Christmas Time represents a period of reflection, generosity, and spiritual renewal that Scrooge resolves to honor throughout the year. | Christmas Time represents a season of joy, redemption, and goodwill that Scrooge embraces after his spiritual experiences.

The Past.name: The Past
The Past.description: The Past is one of the three temporal dimensions Scrooge commits to embracing, representing memories and lessons from his earlier life.

The Present.name: The Present
The Present.description: The Present is one of the three temporal dimensions Scrooge commits to embracing, representing his current actions and relationships.

The Future.name: The Future
The Future.description: The Future is one of the three temporal dimensions Scrooge commits to embracing, representing the potential outcomes of his changed behavior.

The Spirits of All Three.name: The Spirits of All Three
The Spirits of All Three.description: The Spirits of All Three refer to the collective supernatural forces representing Past, Present, and Future, which Scrooge vows to let guide his transformation.

Wandering Spirits.name: Wandering Spirits
Wandering Spirits.description: Wandering Spirits are ethereal beings observed by Scrooge through a window, representing the broader realm of supernatural forces influencing his redemption.

Church Bells.name: Church Bells
Church Bells.description: Church Bells are physical objects whose ringing signals the passage of time and amplifies Scrooge's sense of joy and renewal.

Gruel Saucepan.name: Gruel Saucepan
Gruel Saucepan.description: The Gruel Saucepan is a household object that Scrooge recognizes as part of his former life, now symbolizing his changed circumstances.

Stockings.name: Stockings
Stockings.description: Stockings are clothing items that Scrooge mismanages in his ecstatic frenzy, illustrating his emotional upheaval and renewed vitality.

Golden Sunlight.name: Golden Sunlight
Golden Sunlight.description: Golden Sunlight is a natural phenomenon Scrooge observes after his transformation, symbolizing clarity, hope, and a renewed world.

Heavenly Sky.name: Heavenly Sky
Heavenly Sky.description: Heavenly Sky is the clear, bright sky Scrooge sees upon opening his window, reflecting his inner transformation and joy.

Sweet Fresh Air.name: Sweet Fresh Air
Sweet Fresh Air.description: Sweet Fresh Air is the crisp, invigorating atmosphere Scrooge experiences after his spiritual journey, signifying renewal and purity.

Prize Turkey.name: Prize Turkey
Prize Turkey.description: The prize turkey is a large, notable bird hanging at the poulterer's shop, selected by Scrooge to be delivered anonymously to Bob Cratchit's home.

Poulterer's Shop.name: Poulterer's Shop
Poulterer's Shop.description: The poulterer's shop is a retail location in the next street but one, where the prize turkey is displayed and purchased by Scrooge's instructions.

Boy.name: Boy
Boy.description: The boy is a young street urchin in Sunday clothes whom Scrooge engages to purchase the turkey and deliver instructions to the poulterer. | The boy is a young individual whom Scrooge recompenses, likely for helping with the turkey purchase or delivery.

Knocker.name: Knocker
Knocker.description: The knocker is the door knocker on Scrooge's house, which he suddenly notices and affectionately praises after his transformation.

Turkey.name: Turkey
Turkey.description: The turkey is a large bird purchased by Scrooge, considered too big to carry to Camden Town without a cab.

Cab.name: Cab
Cab.description: A cab is hired by Scrooge to transport the turkey to Camden Town.

Portly Gentleman.name: Portly Gentleman
Portly Gentleman.description: The portly gentleman is a visitor who previously approached Scrooge at his counting house to request a charitable donation, and is now met by Scrooge with unexpected warmth.

Shaving.name: Shaving
Shaving.description: Shaving is a daily grooming activity that Scrooge performs with difficulty due to his shaking hand, yet he remains determined to complete it.

Sticking-Plaster.name: Sticking-Plaster
Sticking-Plaster.description: A sticking-plaster is a small adhesive bandage Scrooge imagines using if he had accidentally cut his nose while shaving.

Scrooge's Niece by Marriage.name: Scrooge's Niece by Marriage
Scrooge's Niece by Marriage.description: Scrooge's niece by marriage is present at Fred's dinner, and is startled when Scrooge enters the dining room unexpectedly.

Christmas Bowl of Smoking Bishop.name: Christmas Bowl of Smoking Bishop
Christmas Bowl of Smoking Bishop.description: A festive drink referenced by Scrooge as part of his gesture to celebrate Christmas and discuss Bob Cratchit's affairs.

Coal Scuttle.name: Coal Scuttle
Coal Scuttle.description: An object Scrooge instructs Bob Cratchit to purchase, indicating improved working conditions and warmth in the office.

Salary Increase.name: Salary Increase
Salary Increase.description: The decision by Scrooge to raise Bob Cratchit's pay, symbolizing a shift from miserliness to generosity.

Total-Abstinence Principle.name: Total-Abstinence Principle
Total-Abstinence Principle.description: The Total-Abstinence Principle is a lifestyle adopted by Scrooge after his transformation, implying he abstains from any form of spiritual or moral excess, particularly related to his past behavior.
